request.question: Which arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia whose close living relatives are the wombats does Springwood State High School have?

title: Guan (bird)
Spotted elachura
Springwood State High School
Wakaleo vanderleuri
Koala
Rhagophthalmidae
Palorchestes
Centenary Heights State High School
Didunculus
Itatiella
sentences: The guans are a number of bird genera which make up the largest group in the family Cracidae.
They are found mainly in northern South America, southern Central America, and a few adjacent Caribbean islands.
There is also the peculiar horned guan ("Oreophasis derbianus") which is not a true guan, but a very distinct and ancient cracid with no close living relatives (Pereira "et al." 2002).
The spotted elachura or spotted wren-babbler ("Elachura formosa") is a species of passerine bird found in the forests of the eastern Himalayas and Southeast Asia.

In the past it was included in the babbler genus "Spelaeornis" as "S. formosus", but molecular phylogenetic studies in 2014 provided evidence that it was distinct from the babblers and part of a basal lineage (one that diverged early) with no other close living relatives within the passerine bird clade Passerida.
This led to the creation of a new family, Elachuridae, to accommodate just one species (a monotypic taxon).
Springwood State High School is a Queensland state school located in the Logan City suburb of Springwood.
The school opened in 1977 to Grade 8 students with its official opening in 1978.
Springwood State High School is situated on 55 acre of bushland and includes a lagoon which is used by senior Biology students.
In addition to this, the school is home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds.
Wakaleo vanderleuri is a species of marsupial lion that lived in Australia during the Miocene (about 16 to 10million years ago).
Being a marsupial, it is not closely related to true cats; its closest living relatives are wombats and the koala.
This diprotodont is hypothesized to have been an omnivore, eating significant amounts of meat, perhaps more like a bear in diet and habit than like an actual lion.
It lacked large canine-like fangs, producing some controversy regarding just how much it actually preyed on living animals.
The meat-eating hypothesis is based largely upon the morphology of its molar teeth.
The species is believed to have been able to hunt by leaping from trees onto its prey.
The koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus", or, inaccurately, koala bear ) is an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia.
It is the only extant representative of the family Phascolarctidae and its closest living relatives are the wombats.
The koala is found in coastal areas of the mainland's eastern and southern regions, inhabiting Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia.
It is easily recognisable by its stout, tailless body and large head with round, fluffy ears and large, spoon-shaped nose.
The koala has a body length of 60 – and weighs 4 – .
Pelage colour ranges from silver grey to chocolate brown.
Koalas from the northern populations are typically smaller and lighter in colour than their counterparts further south.
These populations possibly are separate subspecies, but this is disputed.
The Rhagophthalmidae are a family of beetles within the larger Elateroidea group that include click-beetles.

Members of this beetle family have bioluminescent organs on the larvae and are closely related to the Phengodidae (glowworm beetles) and Lampyridae (fireflies), and were often included in one of these families as a subfamily—Rhagophthalminae.
Towards the end of the 20th century, they were increasingly seen as a distinct family.
But they might be the only reasonably close living relatives of the mysterious fireflies of genus "Pterotus", and could eventually end up in a Lampyridae subfamily with these.
They were mainly included in the Phengodidae for having similar adaptation for displaying glowing lights.
However, this seems to be due to convergent evolution.

If they indeed belong in the Lampyridae, they are related to a group of families that have either a distinct type of flashing lights (Luciolinae) or rely exclusively or nearly so on pheromones for communication (the others).
Palorchestes ('ancient leaper or dancer') is an extinct genus of terrestrial herbivorous marsupial of the family Palorchestidae.
The genus was endemic to Australia, living from the Late Miocene subepoch through the Pleistocene epoch (around 11.6 mya – 11,000 years ago), and thought to be in existence for approximately .

Centenary Heights State High School is a State High School located in the suburb of Centenary Heights in Toowoomba, Queensland, with an enrolment of around 1500 students (2015) and was one of Queensland's leading high schools with 11 OP's 1 (Overall Position) scored in 2012.
The school is one of several state high schools in Toowoomba, including Toowoomba State High School , Harristown State High School and Wilsonton State High School.
The tooth-billed pigeons are the only genus ("Didunculus") of the subfamily Didunculinae, in the pigeon and dove family, (Columbidae).
It has no close living relatives, but it has been shown to be genetically close to the dodo, and the genus name "Didunculus" means "little dodo".

The jaw and tongue structure, and the superficially parrotlike bill have suggested a relationship to the parrots, but these features have arisen from its specialised diet rather than any real relationship.
Itatiella ulei is a species of moss in the family Polytrichaceae.
It is the only species in the genus Itatiella.
The Polytrichaceae is a common family of mosses that does not have close living relatives.
Its small size and the inflexed leaf apex characterize "Itatiella ulei".
When this species grows directly exposed to sun at high elevations, it presents a similar aspect but can be distinguished based on the distal lamella cells which are single and rhombic.

candidate.summarize1.predict: Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Which arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia whose close living relatives are the wombats does Springwood State High School have?
hop1_docs: Wakaleo | Wakaleo (indigenous Australian "waka", "little", "small", and Latin "leo", "lion") was a genus of medium-sized thylacoleonids that lived in Australia in the early to late Miocene.  It was approximately 2.5 ft (80 cm) long, or the size of a dog.  Although much smaller than its close relative, the marsupial lion "(Thylacoleo carnifex)", "Wakaleo" would have been a successful hunter in its time.  It had teeth specially designed for cutting and stabbing.  The ocelot-sized predator "Wakaleo", along with its jaguar-sized relative "Thylacoleo", were actually related to the herbivore wombats.

Koala | The koala ("Phascolarctos cinereus", or, inaccurately, koala bear ) is an arboreal herbivorous marsupial native to Australia.  It is the only extant representative of the family Phascolarctidae and its closest living relatives are the wombats.  The koala is found in coastal areas of the mainland's eastern and southern regions, inhabiting Queensland, New South Wales, Victoria, and South Australia.  It is easily recognisable by its stout, tailless body and large head with round, fluffy ears and large, spoon-shaped nose.  The koala has a body length of 60 – and weighs 4 – .  Pelage colour ranges from silver grey to chocolate brown.  Koalas from the northern populations are typically smaller and lighter in colour than their counterparts further south.  These populations possibly are separate subspecies, but this is disputed.

Wakaleo vanderleuri | Wakaleo vanderleuri is a species of marsupial lion that lived in Australia during the Miocene (about 16 to 10million years ago).  Being a marsupial, it is not closely related to true cats; its closest living relatives are wombats and the koala.  This diprotodont is hypothesized to have been an omnivore, eating significant amounts of meat, perhaps more like a bear in diet and habit than like an actual lion.  It lacked large canine-like fangs, producing some controversy regarding just how much it actually preyed on living animals.  The meat-eating hypothesis is based largely upon the morphology of its molar teeth.  The species is believed to have been able to hunt by leaping from trees onto its prey.

Springwood State High School | Springwood State High School is a Queensland state school located in the Logan City suburb of Springwood.  The school opened in 1977 to Grade 8 students with its official opening in 1978.  Springwood State High School is situated on 55 acre of bushland and includes a lagoon which is used by senior Biology students.  In addition to this, the school is home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds.
Muramura | "Muramura williamsi" is an extinct marsupial, related to the modern koala and wombat.  Around the size of a dog, it was a herbivore.
Ngapakaldia | Ngapakaldia is an extinct genus of marsupial, related to the modern koala and wombat.  Around the size of a sheep, it was a ground-dwelling herbivore.

Phalangeridae | The Phalangeridae are a family of nocturnal marsupials native to Australia, New Guinea and Eastern Indonesia, including the cuscuses, brushtail possums, and their close relatives.  Considered a type of possum, most species are arboreal, and they inhabit a wide range of forest habitats from alpine woodland to eucalypt forest and tropical jungle.
hop2_docs: Springwood State High School | Springwood State High School is a Queensland state school located in the Logan City suburb of Springwood.  The school opened in 1977 to Grade 8 students with its official opening in 1978.  Springwood State High School is situated on 55 acre of bushland and includes a lagoon which is used by senior Biology students.  In addition to this, the school is home to many native animal species including Koalas, Wallabies and many species of birds.
Springwood High School (Alabama) | Springwood High School, located in Lanett, Alabama, United States, is a private school serving around 300 students in grades K4-12.  Founded in 1970.

Springwood High School, King's Lynn | Springwood High School is a secondary school with academy status in the coastal town of King's Lynn in Norfolk, England.  It was formed by the merger of several schools when the government began to abolish the tripartite system in the mid-1960s.  Springwood has a Sixth Form with over 300 pupils and has been designated a specialist arts college and teacher training school.

Springwood High School (New South Wales) | Springwood High School is a public high school situated in the City of Blue Mountains, New South Wales.  Despite its name, the school is actually located in Grose Road, Faulconbridge.  The school was established in 1967.  Its motto is "Press Onward."

James River High School (Buchanan, Virginia) | James River High School (JRHS) is a Group-2A-size public high school in Buchanan, Virginia.  It serves the northern part of Botetourt County, including the towns of Buchanan, Eagle Rock, Fincastle, and Springwood.  James River was founded in 1959 with the consolidation of the Buchanan, Eagle Rock, and Fincastle high schools.

St Columba's High School, New South Wales | St Columba's High School, or St Columba's Catholic College, is a Roman Catholic, co-educational, secondary day school, located on the border of Winmalee and Springwood, in New South Wales, Australia.

Springwood, Queensland | Springwood is a suburb of Logan City, Queensland, Australia.  The size of Springwood is approximately 6 km².  It has 17 parks covering nearly 9% of the total area.  The population of Springwood in 1996 was 9,738 people.  By 2001 the population was 9,084 showing a population decline of 7% in the area during that time.  A high proportion of people in Springwood are in the baby boomers generation compared to population proportions in the surrounding local government area.
hop2_titles: St Columba's High School, New South Wales
